name: New Jersey Attorney General / Dept of Law & Public Safety
tagline: New Jersey's chief law-enforcement department, led by the Attorney General: a sprawling agency that houses the State Police, Division of Criminal Justice, Juvenile Justice, and Consumer Affairs, with deep public-safety IT, intelligence, digital-evidence, and cyber needs.
overview: The New Jersey Department of Law & Public Safety (LPS) is the state's chief law-enforcement and legal-affairs department, led by the Attorney General as New Jersey's top law-enforcement officer. It is one of the largest and most technology-intensive departments in state government, housing the New Jersey State Police, the Division of Criminal Justice, the Juvenile Justice Commission, the Division of Consumer Affairs, the Division on Civil Rights, the Office of the Insurance Fraud Prosecutor, and more. The department employs roughly 8,000 people and the Attorney General sets policy and standards for the 42,000-plus state and local law-enforcement officers across New Jersey. For a Dell SLG seller this is a high-value, multi-program account that spans field and mobile (patrol vehicles, in-car and body-worn systems, mobile data computers), secure data-center and storage (records management, criminal-justice data, digital evidence), real-time intelligence and analytics (the State Police Regional Operations & Intelligence Center / fusion center), forensics and biometrics, and cybersecurity. The account sits inside a fresh leadership reset: Attorney General Jennifer Davenport took office in January 2026 under Governor Mikie Sherrill and was confirmed by the State Senate in February 2026, so priorities and buying-center contacts are being re-established. Note that central IT, the state data center, and statewide cyber strategy run outside LPS at NJOIT, while NJCCIC (the state's cyber threat-sharing cell) sits with the Office of Homeland Security & Preparedness — LPS owns its own operational, CJIS-governed public-safety systems.

budget.intro: LPS is a large direct spender funded through the state budget, with the New Jersey State Police as the biggest cost center inside the department. Governor Sherrill signed her inaugural FY2027 budget (~$60.7B statewide) into law on June 30, 2026; the LPS appropriation is a line within it, and the Legislature's LPS budget analysis recommends about $515.6M in Direct State Services for the Division of State Police in FY2027 (up ~$1.6M over FY2026), funding roughly 4,668 State Police full-time positions. Confirm the enacted department and division totals against the Governor's budget documents and the Legislature's LPS analysis before forecasting.

stats.label: FY2027 state budget (signed June 30, 2026)
stats.note: Sherrill's first budget; LPS appropriation is a line within it

stats.label: State Police Direct State Services (FY2027)
stats.note: Largest cost center in LPS; ~$1.6M above FY2026, funding ~4,668 FTEs

stats.label: Officers overseen
stats.note: State and local law-enforcement officers under AG policy/standards

notes: The State Police is the largest cost center within LPS; the Division of Criminal Justice, Juvenile Justice, and Consumer Affairs are other significant operating units.

Exact LPS and State Police appropriations are in the FY2027 Governor's budget and the Legislature's Department of Law & Public Safety budget analysis / discussion points — verify the enacted figure (set around the July 1 fiscal-year start).

Significant technology funding also arrives outside the operating line — federal homeland-security and justice grants, and the prior $58M statewide body-worn-camera grant program administered by the AG's Office — which drive recurring storage and digital-evidence demand.

initiatives.name: Body-worn cameras & digital-evidence management
initiatives.summary: New Jersey requires patrol officers statewide to wear body cameras (AG directive), and the AG's Office administered roughly $58M in grants to equip nearly 500 agencies. The state deliberately chose not to build one central footage repository, leaving large, recurring needs for secure storage, retention, redaction, and digital-evidence management — a clear infrastructure and storage opening.

initiatives.name: State Police CAD/RMS & mobile modernization
initiatives.summary: The State Police run statewide computer-aided dispatch (CAD), records management (RMS), and mobile data computers for field operations. These mission-critical, CJIS-governed systems are periodic modernization tracks that map to server, storage, resiliency, and ruggedized edge/mobile compute.

initiatives.name: ROIC fusion center & real-time intelligence/AI
initiatives.summary: The NJ Regional Operations & Intelligence Center (ROIC) is the state's primary hub for information-sharing, threat analysis, and homeland-security intelligence, partnering with federal, state, and local agencies. Real-time data ingestion, analytics, and AI for threat detection map directly to high-performance compute, storage, and secure-data-center needs.

initiatives.name: Forensics, biometrics & criminal-justice data
initiatives.summary: The State Police lab and the Division of Criminal Justice run forensic science, fingerprint/biometric identification, and criminal-history systems under CJIS rules. Modernizing these data-heavy, high-availability systems is an opening for secure storage, disaster recovery, and compute.

procurement.intro: LPS buys through New Jersey's central procurement framework, not primarily one-off agency RFPs. The contracting path runs through the Treasury Division of Purchase & Property (DPP) and the NJSTART eProcurement portal, with NJOIT shared services for IT and cooperative purchasing extending awards to local agencies. Two preconditions: an active, correctly categorized NJSTART vendor registration, and the right DPP statewide term contract for the offering. Anything touching criminal-justice data carries CJIS security obligations.

vehicles.name: NJSTART vendor registration (DPP)
vehicles.detail: The mandatory front door — vendors register and maintain a NJSTART profile to bid, hold contracts, and invoice electronically. Confirm Dell's registration and categories are current.

vehicles.name: DPP statewide term contracts
vehicles.detail: Treasury's Division of Purchase & Property awards master/term contracts agencies (including LPS) buy off without a separate RFP — IT-relevant categories include computer equipment, cloud/software, and IT consulting. Verify exact contract numbers and scope before forecasting.

vehicles.name: NJOIT shared services & cooperatives
vehicles.detail: Central IT, data-center, and shared services run through NJOIT; national cooperative vehicles (e.g., NASPO ValuePoint) and the NJ Cooperative Purchasing Program extend state awards to counties, municipalities, and authorities.

vehicles.name: CJIS compliance
vehicles.detail: Criminal-justice systems (records, biometrics, digital evidence) must meet FBI CJIS Security Policy requirements — a hard constraint on any solution touching LPS/State Police data.

powerStructure.name: Jennifer Davenport
powerStructure.title: Attorney General
powerStructure.note: Took office Jan 2026 under Gov. Sherrill; confirmed unanimously by the State Senate Feb 24, 2026; still serving as of June 2026 (co-launched the June 2026 statewide junk-fees enforcement initiative). Former first assistant AG and federal prosecutor. Sets department direction and statewide law-enforcement policy — the top relationship; the LPS leadership reset is now largely staffed.

powerStructure.name: Jeanne Hengemuhle
powerStructure.title: Superintendent, NJ State Police (acting; nominated Feb 2026, pending Senate confirmation)
powerStructure.note: Sherrill's pick to lead the State Police — the department's biggest operational and technology footprint (field/mobile, CAD/RMS, ROIC, forensics). Assumed the acting role Feb 26, 2026 (first woman to lead the force in its history), succeeding interim Lt. Col. David Sierotowicz; a 26-year NJSP veteran who retired as lieutenant colonel in 2024. Confirm Senate confirmation status before engagement.

powerStructure.name: Director, Division of Criminal Justice
powerStructure.title: Statewide prosecution & criminal-justice data
powerStructure.note: Leads criminal investigations/prosecutions and oversight of county prosecutors — a buying center for case, evidence, and analytics systems.

powerStructure.name: LPS / State Police IT leadership
powerStructure.title: Department & State Police technology
powerStructure.note: The State Police Identification & Information Technology Section and LPS IT own operational public-safety systems and CJIS security. The primary infrastructure decision-makers inside the department — confirm current named CIO/IT directors.

powerStructure.name: Kevin Dehmer
powerStructure.title: Chief Technology Officer, NJOIT
powerStructure.note: Appointed Feb 2026; runs central IT, data center, and statewide cyber strategy OUTSIDE LPS. Relevant because shared services and statewide policy flow through NJOIT even as LPS owns its operational systems.

news.title: Sherrill signs first FY2027 budget (~$60.7B) June 30; State Police Division funded at ~$515.6M Direct State Services
news.impact: Locks in the department's enacted funding envelope: the State Police operating line rises ~$1.6M over FY2026 but the budget funds ~4,668 State Police FTEs (about 63 fewer than were filled in Jan 2026) under an affordability/efficiency frame — pace any forecast to the enacted line, not the March proposal, and watch for modernization dollars inside grants rather than headcount.

news.title: Sherrill names Jeanne Hengemuhle first woman to lead NJ State Police (acting Superintendent, pending Senate confirmation)
news.impact: New leadership atop the department's largest operational and technology footprint (field/mobile, CAD/RMS, ROIC, forensics) — re-map the State Police buying center and confirm confirmation status before engaging on infrastructure tracks.

news.title: Jennifer Davenport confirmed by State Senate as NJ Attorney General
news.impact: Confirms new top-of-house leadership at LPS after taking office in January — reset the executive relationship map across the department and its divisions.

news.title: Davenport sworn in as Acting Attorney General under the Sherrill administration
news.impact: Leadership transition at the head of the state's largest law-enforcement department — re-confirm priorities and contacts in State Police, Criminal Justice, and IT.

news.title: NJCCIC marks 10 years as the nation's first state cyber threat-sharing cell (NJOHSP, co-located at the ROIC)
news.impact: Underscores NJ's mature cyber/intelligence ecosystem around the State Police ROIC — relevant for analytics and secure-infrastructure positioning, though NJCCIC sits with NJOHSP, not LPS.

dellActions: Re-map the executive and IT buying centers after the Jan/Feb 2026 transition under AG Jennifer Davenport and new (acting) State Police Superintendent Jeanne Hengemuhle; confirm her Senate confirmation status and the current LPS/State Police IT leadership before forecasting.

Lead with digital-evidence and body-worn-camera storage: NJ chose not to centralize footage, leaving recurring secure storage, retention, redaction, and DR demand across the AG-administered program — a concrete entry point.
Position secure data-center, storage, high-availability, and DR for mission-critical State Police systems (CAD/RMS, forensics/biometrics, criminal-history) under CJIS requirements.
Pursue AI/analytics and high-performance compute for the ROIC fusion center's real-time intelligence and threat-detection mission, plus ruggedized edge/mobile for field operations.

Confirm Dell's NJSTART registration and the right DPP statewide term contracts as the access path, use NJOIT shared services and cooperative vehicles to broaden reach, and clear CJIS obligations early for any data-touching play.
